coredata.dc:title: Enhancing reducing ability of α-zein by fibrillation for synthesis of Au nanocrystals with continuous flow catalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yijun
Wu, Xiaochen
Lv, Lili
Li, Fei
Liu, Zhengqin
Kong, Qingshan
Li, Chaoxu
coredata.dc:description: Green low-cost synthesis and efficient recyclability are two major hindrances for Au nanocrystals as catalysts applying in diverse industrial reaction processes. By the use of low-cost α-zein (i.e. a major storage protein of corn) as the reductant, capping agent and stabilizer, Au nanocrystals with tunable catalytic activity were synthesized in a wet-chemical approach. Fibrillation of α-zein further enhanced its reducing ability due to larger specific surface area and more hydrophilic groups exposed on the surfaces. The obtained Au nanocrystals had biocompatibility, high stability in various solvents, unique solubility in aqueous alcohol and high catalytic ability, being able to detect ethanol composition in aqueous ethanol as well as H2O2 for diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. These advantages also enable efficient recyclability of Au nanocrystals with continuous flow catalysis in different solvents and environments. Thus, the use of α-zein offered Au nanocrystals not only with green low-cost synthesis, but also with tunable catalytic activities, ethanol-responsiveness and efficient recyclability, which may be applicable in diverse fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofibrils
Au nanocrystals
α-Zein
Catalytic activity
Flow catalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical characterization of Au nanocrystals synthesized by the assistance of α-zein. (a) UV–vis spectrophotometry of Auzein-0.4 with different synthesis time. The inset gives visual observation under different synthesis time. (b–e) TEM images of Auzein-0.05 (b), Auzein-0.1 (c), Auzein-0.2 (d) and Auzein-0.4 (e). The scale bars are 200nm. (f) Lateral size distributions.

(a) Schematic illustration of fibrillation process of α-zein. (b) Exampled TEM image of α-zein nanofibrils. The inset gives visual observation of the solutions of α-zein and its nanofibrils. (c) ThT fluorescence of α-zein fibrils with native α-zein and water as reference. Experimental parameters: 0.2wt.% α-zein, 70vol.% aqueous ethanol, pH 8.0, 60°C, 48h.

Physical characterizations of Au nanocrystals synthesized by the assistance of α-zein nanofibrils. (a) UV–vis spectrophotometry of Aufibril-0.4 with different synthesis time. The inset gives visual observation under different synthesis time. (b) UV–vis absorbance at 560nm for Aufibril-0.4 and at 590nm for Auzein-0.4 against synthesis time. (c) Position comparison of UV–vis absorption maximum between Aufibril-x and Auzein-x synthesized with different concentration of α-zein.

(a–c) TEM images of Au nanocrystals synthesized with α-zein nanofibrils. (a) Aufibril-0.1, (b) Aufibril-0.2 and (c) Aufibril-0.4. The scale bars are 100nm. (d) Lateral size distributions of Au nanocrystals synthesized with α-zein nanofibrils.
(a) Visual observation of Aufibril-0.4 suspended in different concentrations of ethanol. (b) UV–vis absorption of Aufibril-0.4 suspended in different concentrations of ethanol.

(a) Time-dependent UV–vis absorbance at 650nm of TMB catalyzed by Auzein-x and Aufibril-x in the presence of H2O2. Experimental parameters: 3mM H2O2, 0.9mM TMB, pH 3.6. (b) UV–vis absorption at 650nm of TMB catalyzed by Aufibril-0.4 in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2. The inset gives visual observation of the reaction. Experimental parameters: Aufibril-0.4 (8.1μg of net Au weight), 0.9mM of TMB, pH 3.6.

(a) SEM images of Aufibril-0.4 deposited on filtering membrane. The inset gives visual observation of the membrane (left top). (b) Schematic representation of flow catalysis of Aufibril-0.4 membrane for organic dye. (c) UV–vis absorbance of methylene blue before and after passing through Aufibril-0.4 membrane. Experimental parameters: 10−4 M methylene blue, 2mM NaBH4.

head.ce:title: Enhancing reducing ability of α-zein by fibrillation for synthesis of Au nanocrystals with continuous flow catalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-based materials, Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy and Bioprocess Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Songling Road 189, Qingdao 266101, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-based materials
Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy and Bioprocess Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yijun Chen and Xiaochen Wu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green low-cost synthesis and efficient recyclability are two major hindrances for Au nanocrystals as catalysts applying in diverse industrial reaction processes. By the use of low-cost α-zein (i.e. a major storage protein of corn) as the reductant, capping agent and stabilizer, Au nanocrystals with tunable catalytic activity were synthesized in a wet-chemical approach. Fibrillation of α-zein further enhanced its reducing ability due to larger specific surface area and more hydrophilic groups exposed on the surfaces. The obtained Au nanocrystals had biocompatibility, high stability in various solvents, unique solubility in aqueous alcohol and high catalytic ability, being able to detect ethanol composition in aqueous ethanol as well as H2O2 for diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. These advantages also enable efficient recyclability of Au nanocrystals with continuous flow catalysis in different solvents and environments. Thus, the use of α-zein offered Au nanocrystals not only with green low-cost synthesis, but also with tunable catalytic activities, ethanol-responsiveness and efficient recyclability, which may be applicable in diverse fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to an exceptional combination of super chemical stability, unique atomic structure and quantum size effect, nanocrystals of noble metals have promised a variety of applications in sensing, electronics, optics and biomedicines [1–5]. In particular, Au nanocrystals may have their catalytic activity and selectivity finely tailored through their sizes and morphologies, enabling one of the most attractive catalysts for diverse chemical and electrochemical reactions [6–9]. For example, in sharp contrast to catalytic inactivity of bulk Au, Au nanocrystals have high catalytic activity to CO oxidation and propylene epoxidation even at the temperatures lower than 0°C [10]. In order to efficiently cut down the cost of various reactions, recent endeavor has been made mainly on low-cost synthesis of Au catalysts as well as their efficient recyclability.

Among the diverse well-developed synthesis approaches of Au nanocrystals [11,12], green wet-chemical methods involving natural or biodegradable chemicals attract particular interests due to their environmental friendliness, biocompatibility and low-cost [13]. For example, Au nanoparticles synthesized with bovine serum albumin showed almost no hemolytic and cytotoxic responses, being an excellent candidate for drug delivery vehicles [14]. Many water-soluble biological molecules, e.g. protein [15], enzyme [16], amino acids [17], sodium alginate [18] and cellulose [19], have been used. Following the nucleation-growth mechanism, HAuCl4 may be reduced into Au nuclei by these molecules, which further guide the growth into different sizes and morphologies [13]. In principle, smaller sizes were preferred by a fast reducing process. When these molecules selectively absorb on certain crystallographic facets of Au nanocrystals, the morphologies can be tuned as sphere, triangle, hexagon, belt, etc.

In terms of catalyst recovery, conventional methods such as filtration and centrifugation normally become invalid in the case of Au nanocrystals. Instead, Au nanocrystals were embedded into inexpensive carrying systems (e.g. polymers [20], surfactants [21] and colloids [6]) with no coagulation and blocking their active catalytic surfaces. When a filtering membrane acts as the carrying scaffold, continuous flow catalysis occurs with simultaneous flow and catalysis [4,7], being in analogue to industrial flow reacting processes. In spite of great progress in continuous flow catalysis, an efficient system remains challenging with respect to tunable catalytic activity, firm immobilization of catalysts on membranes and endurance to different solvents and environments.

In this study we showed that α-zein, one of the most widely available proteins, could be used to synthesize Au nanocrystals as a reducing agent, capping agent and stabilizer. Its reducing ability could be further enhanced by a self-fibrillation process, therefore benefiting to synthesize Au nanocrystals with tunable catalytic activity. The resultant Au nanocrystals were firmly immobilized on α-zein nanofibrils, forming a unique type of hybrid for continuous flow catalysis. High aspect ratio and structural rigidity of these α-zein nanofibrils enable facile deposition of Au nanocrystals on filtering membranes without the risk of passing through the filter pores with larger sizes. α-Zein has poor solubility in most of solvents, further offering this flow catalyst with wide applicability under various harsh conditions. Also we showed that this hybrid combined the solubility of α-zein in 60–90vol.% ethanol and high catalysis activity of Au nanocrystals, being potentially used for detection of ethanol and H2O2.

Beside these, this study has also the novelties from the following points of view. First, instead of previous water-soluble and expensive proteins [13,22], α-zein, one of the most widely available proteins in nature, was used to synthesize Au nanocrystals. α-Zein is a major storage protein of corn with the molecular weight of 19–22kDa. Having roughly two-thirds of hydrophobic and one-third of hydrophilic amino acid residues, it shows insolubility in most solvents except aqueous alcohol with proper compositions (60–90vol.%) of alcohol. Due to its natural abundance, extractability, unique solubility, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, it has been widely applied in packaging, food, pharmaceutical and cosmetic fields [23,24]. However, its roles in synthesis of Au nanocrystals have not been well revealed [15]. Secondly, differing from relatively lower specific area of electro-spinning fibers as scaffolds of Au nanocrystals [25,26], β-sheets based nanofibrils of α-zein have much smaller diameters and hereby high specific area to support Au catalysts. Moreover, they inherit both the stability of α-zein under different conditions and mechanic properties of β-sheets based architectures [27], in contrast to the relatively lower stability of amyloid fibrils made of water-soluble proteins (e.g. β-lactoglobulin and lysozyme) in organic solvents and high ionic strength conditions [5,22]. The enhanced reducing ability of α-zein nanofibrils further endows the possibility to tune the sizes of Au nanocrystals immobilizing on themselves. All these advantages may expect to expand the application scenarios of flow catalysis and α-zein. In the end, by using α-zein nanofibrils as a unique model, the effect of α-zein assemblies on their reducing ability was investigated for the first time.

α-Zein was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4, 48% Au basis) was purchased from Sigma. All other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and used without further purification.

α-Zein was dissolved into aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol) under vigorously stirring and adjusted the concentration within 0.1–0.4wt.% at pH∼8. The resultant solution was then incubated at 60°C for 48h for fibrillation of α-zein.

α-Zein assisted synthesis of Au nanocrystals: α-zein was dissolved into aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol) to prepare homogeneous solutions with the concentration within 0.05–0.4wt.%. After adding 200μL HAuCl4 (24.4mM), the solutions (10mL) were incubated at 70°C and pH∼4 for 72h. The resultant Au nanocrystals were washed three times with aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol) through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 15min. The sample code Auzein-X was given as Auzein-0.05, Auzein-0.1, Auzein-0.2 and Auzein-0.4, where X refers to the initial concentration of α-zein (unit: wt.%) for synthesis of Au nanocrystals.

α-Zein nanofibrils assisted synthesis of Au nanocrystals: 200μL of HAuCl4 (24.4mM) was added into the solution of α-zein nanofibrils (10mL, 0.1–0.4wt.%). After incubation at 70°C and pH∼4 for 24h, the resultant Au nanocrystals were washed three times with aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol) through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 15min. The sample code Aufibril-X was given as Aufibril-0.1, Aufibril-0.2 and Aufibril-0.4, where X refers to the initial concentration of α-zein nanofibrils (unit: wt.%) for synthesis of Au nanocrystals.

Catalytic reaction of H2O2 : 8.1μg Au nanocrystals was added into 1mL NaAc buffer (0.1M, pH 3.6) with 0.9mM 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 3mM hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in a 2mL quartz cuvette. The mixture was measured in time course mode by monitoring the absorbance change at 650nm for 480s on a UV–vis spectrophotometer at room temperature. Then, a series of experiments were performed by changing the concentration of H2O2 or α-zein decorated Au nanocrystals to characterize their catalytic ability.

Catalytic degradation of dyes: The solution Aufibril-0.4 was vacuum-filtered on a nitrocellulose membrane (diameter 12mm). Then 5mL of methylene blue (10−4 M) and NaBH4 (10−1 M) mixture were filtered through the catalytic membrane. The filtrate was analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a Hitachi TEM (H-7650) instrument operating at a voltage of 100kV. The samples were prepared by dropping the solutions on carbon-coated Cu grids followed by air-drying. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed on a Hitachi S-4800 operated at 10kV. Nano S90 nano laser granulometer (Malvern) was used to measure size distributions of colloidal Au nanocrystals. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed on a DU800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The solutions were diluted and scanned in 1-cm-pathlength quarts cuvettes at 480nm/min. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an F-4600 spectrofluorometer with excitation at 440nm. Thioflavin T (ThT) binding assays were conducted by adding 200μL ThT solution (20mM) to the solution of α-zein fibrils (2.8mL and 0.4wt.%).

Many water-soluble proteins (e.g. bovine serum albumin [14]) and amino acids [17] were reported to have the capability of reducing HAuCl4. In spite of lacking aqueous solubility, amphiphilic α-zein was also found to be capable of reducing HAuCl4 to Au nanocrystals in aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol). As shown in Fig.1 a, upon adding AuCl4 − into the solution of α-zein (0.4wt.%), the solution became a little pink at 70°C in 24h. Correspondingly, there appeared a UV–vis absorption maximum around ∼592nm which intensified with time. The reducing process depended strongly on the concentration of α-zein. Under the same experimental parameters, increasing the concentration of α-zein offers the absorption maximum with red shift (see Fig. S1a). On account of close correlation between Au nanocrystals and their surface plasmon resonance, these varieties of absorption maximum indicated that the resultant Au nanocrystals had larger sizes [28]. To be noted, much weaker reducing ability of ethanol could be excluded in the present study.

The larger sizes of Au nanocrystals are in perfect agreement with the TEM characterization results. As shown in Fig.1b–e, the synthesized Au nanocrystals exhibited diverse morphologies, e.g. spherical, triangular, hexagonal and irregular. And the average sizes characterized by particle size analyzer increased from 130nm to 225nm when the concentration of α-zein rose from 0.05 to 0.4wt.% (see Fig.1f). Besides serving as the reductant, α-zein could also tune the morphologies of Au nanocrystals as a capping agent. And the concentration 0.1–0.2wt.% seemed to be ideal to maximize the production of regular Au platelets. Both the lower and higher concentrations of α-zein created a large amount of spherical Au nanocrystals.

In principle, most of two-dimensional (2D) Au nanocrystals have the (111) crystallographic planes on the top and bottom planes and are thermodynamically unfavored. “A kinetic controlled pathway” is essential to synthesize 2D Au nanocrystals in the presence of reductants and capping agents [22]. According to the nucleation-growth mechanism, a reductant should be selected with proper reducing activity and concentration to ensure slower reducing processes. And the capping agent could guide 2D growth of Au nanocrystals by selectively absorbing on their (111) planes [29,30]. In this study, when α-zein functionalized as both the reductant and capping agent, spherical Au nanocrystals were produced at lower concentrations of α-zein (e.g. 0.05wt.%) probably due to its weak capping effect. Higher concentrations of α-zein (e.g. 0.4wt.%), though having higher capping effect, greatly accelerated the reducing processes and hereby produced a large amount of spherical Au nanocrystals. It is worth to note that higher reduction rates normally favor the nucleation process of Au nanocrystals rather than their growth process, and hereby produce smaller Au nanocrystals. The opposite tendency in the present case may be resulted from micelle-like molecular aggregations of α-zein in aqueous ethanol, which may become larger at higher concentrations and also offer relatively smaller specific surface area available to reduce HAuCl4 [24].

Recently it was reported that α-zein could undergo a fibrillation process in aqueous ethanol [27]. For example, when incubating at the temperature >60°C and pH>4, micelle-like molecular aggregations of α-zein underwent a slow structural transformation into fibrous structures (see Fig.2 a). These nanofibrils have the apparent diameter within 5–40nm and the aspect ratio up to 103 (Fig.2b). Further structural characterization via ThT binding assays revealed that they shared structural similarities with amyloid fibrillation [31]. In contrast to weak ThT fluorescence of α-zein, there appeared a strong fluorescence signal maximum at 440nm in the suspension of α-zein fibrils, signifying the presence of β–sheets (Fig.2c).

These nanofibrils could maintain the reducing ability of α-zein. As shown in Fig.3 a, the addition of AuCl4 − produced a color variety from transparency to pink. Moreover, though with the same experimental parameters, the solution of nanofibrils (0.4wt.%) has the color variety more evident than that of the α-zein solution in Fig.1a. Correspondingly, the UV–vis absorption maximum appeared at ∼560nm, much smaller than ∼592nm in Fig.1a. The absorption maximum, measured after 6h’s reaction, has the intensity one order of magnitude higher than that of the α-zein system (see Fig.3b), suggesting higher yield of Au nanocrystals. Again in similarity to α-zein, the reducing process depended strongly on the concentration of nanofibrils. Under the same experimental parameters, increasing the concentration of nanofibrils offers a blue shift of the absorption maximum (Figs.3c and S1b), suggesting smaller sizes of Au nanocrystals.

Smaller sizes of Au nanocrystals were produced not only by increasing the concentration of nanofibrils, but also by replacing α-zein with its nanofibrils. These phenomena were confirmed by TEM images in Fig.4 a–c. The average sizes characterized by particle size analyzer decreased from 110nm to 46nm when the concentration of nanofibrils rose from 0.1wt.% to 0.4wt.% (see Fig.4d). In particular, the ideal concentration to maximize the yield of regular Au platelets is 0.1wt.%, in contrast to 0.1–0.2wt.% of α-zein shown in Fig. 1.

With comparison to α-zein, α-zein nanofibrils tend to produce smaller and spherical Au nanocrystals under the same experimental parameters. This may originate in stronger reduction ability of α-zein fibrils than its native state. In aqueous ethanol, native α-zein was dissolved in the form of micelle-like aggregations with hydrophilic corona (see Fig.2a). Its fibrillation not only produced larger specific surface area, but also exposed more hydrophilic groups on their surfaces available to reduce HAuCl4. The difference of specific surface area between native α-zein and its nanofibrils may be promoted at higher concentrations, thus producing larger difference in reducing abilities. It worth to mention that fibrillation of α-zein may occur simultaneously with the reducing process of HAuCl4 in Fig. 1, whose slower kinetics [27], however, would greatly weaken its contribution to the synthesis of Au nanocrystals.

After washing three times with aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol) through centrifugation and re-dispersion, Au nanocrystals synthesized by α-zein showed similar solubility with α-zein. For example, α-zein is capable of dissolving in 60–90vol.% ethanol, and phase separation could be observed at lower or higher ethanol contents (<60 and >90vol.%) [23,32]. Aufibril-0.4 also formed a uniform solution in aqueous ethanol (70vol.% ethanol). Its SPR absorption started to weaken by adding H2O, and completely recovered by tuning the ethanol content back to 70vol.% (Fig. 5 ). This unique feature suggests that Aufibril-0.4 was functionalized successfully by α-zein, being responsive to environmental inputs and possibly used to detect ethanol.

Recyclability is of great importance for Au nanocrystals to be used as catalysts. Differing from aqueous solubility of Au nanocrystals synthesized by normal proteins, Aufibril-0.4 offered the possibility of facilely concentrating Au nanocrystals by adding water (or in combination with centrifugation at low rpm) and then re-dispersing in aqueous ethanol. The re-dispersion process could be repeated many times (see Fig. S2). Also in contrast to aggregation of Au nanocrystals synthesized by normal proteins, Aufibril-0.4 kept stable when exposing to high ionic strength and different ions, such as Na+, NO3 − and Cl− (see Fig. S3).

Catalytic activities of Auzein-X and Aufibril-X were evaluated through oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, in which TMB was a typical chromogenic agent to measure catalytic activities [33]. The reaction process was monitored by using characteristic UV–vis absorption of the reacting product at 650nm (Fig. S4). It can be seen in Fig.6 a that catalytic activities of Au nanocrystals follow the sequence: Aufibril-0.4 >Aufibril-0.2 >Aufibril-0.1 >Auzein-0.05 >Auzein-0.1 >Auzein-0.2 >Auzein-0.4. In terms of the relationship of catalytic activity and the size of gold nanocrystals [34], this sequence is in perfect with the observation in Figs. 1 and 4. That is, higher concentrations of α-zein nanofibrils tend to create smaller sizes of Au nanocrystals.

Due to its high catalytic activity, Aufibril-0.4 can be used to detect H2O2. Again time-dependent absorbance at 650nm was measured against the concentrations of Aufibril-0.4 and H2O2 in the presence of TMB (see Fig. S5 and Fig.6b). When fixing the amount of Aufibril-0.4 (containing 8.1μg Au nanocrystals), the absorption at 650nm showed a steep linear relationship with the concentration of H2O2 before reaching a plateau at 20mM H2O2. The high sensitivity of UV–vis absorption to the H2O2 concentration offers the possibility to detect H2O2 by naked eyes with a limit of 3mM (see the inset of Fig.6b), which may be a promising alternative of glucose detection for diabetes mellitus [8].

The high catalytic activity of Aufibril-0.4 also enables continuous flow catalysis. Owing to the high aspect ratios of α-zein nanofibrils, Aufibril-0.4 could easily deposit on porous membranes by filtering its solution (see Fig.7 a). When a typical dye of methylene blue passed through the membrane, Aufibril-0.4 catalyzed the degradation of methylene blue in the presence of NaBH4 (see Fig.7b) [35–45]. 1mL of 10−4 M methylene blue could be fully degraded after passing through the membrane (in the presence of 20μL of 10−1 M NaBH4), confirming the excellent catalytic activity of Aufibril-0.4 (Fig.7c). Moreover, after several catalytic cycles, the Aufibril-0.4 still maintained the initial structure and morphologies (see the XRD patterns, TEM and SEM images in Fig. S6), again confirmed the good recyclability of Au nanocrystals decorated by α-zein.

Fibrillation of α-zein was found to be able to enhance its reducing ability to synthesize Au nanocrystals. And thus Au nanocrystals could be synthesized with tunable and high catalytic activity by the use of α-zein. The resultant Au nanocrystals inherited the advantages of α-zein in biocompatibility, stability and solubility in aqueous alcohol and could further be used to detect the ethanol composition in aqueous ethanol as well as H2O2 for diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. When depositing on filtering membranes, these Au nanocrystals exhibited continuous flow catalysis with endurance to different solvents and environments. In brief, this unique combination of α-zein offered Au nanocrystals not only with low-cost and green synthesis methods, but also with tunable catalytic activities, ethanol-responsiveness and efficient recyclability, which may be applicable in diverse fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Chinese “1000 youth Talent Program”, National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21474125), National Key Technology R&D Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology (No. 2015BAD14B06) and Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center for marine biomass fiber materials and textiles are kindly acknowledged for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images, TEM image, XRD patterns, lateral size distribution, UV–Vis spectra and catalytic ability of Au nanocrystals synthesized with α-zein and its nanofibrils. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.09.081.
